content.full_text:
Fern Polrrrcar- PRACTTcBs C oMMrssroN
P.O. Box 807 o 428 J Street r Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
August 1,2001
Robert A. Martinez,Board Director
Pico Water District
9573 See Drive
Pico Rivera, CA90660
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-01-171
Dear Mr. Martinez.
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").I Since your advice request does not refer to a specific
governmental decision, but instead seeks general guidance, we are treating your request
as one for informal assistance.2
QUESTIONS
1. Can you hold two public positions at the same time?
2. Does a conflict of interest arise if you become a member of the school board while
you are a member of the board of directors for the Pico Water District?
3. Do the conflict-of-interest rules of the Act apply to school districts?
i Government Code sections 81000 - gIO14. Commission regulations appeff at Title 2, sections
1 8 i 0 9- I 8997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal r,vritten advice. (Section 83114; Regulation I8329(c)(3).)


File No. I-01-171
Page No. 2
CONCLUSIONS
1. There is nothing in the Act that would prohibit you from holding two public positions
concurrently, although other laws may restrict the ability of public offrcials to hold
two different public offices simultaneously if the offices are determined to be
"incompatible offices."
2. A disqualifying conflict of interest only arises under the Act when a public official at
the state or local governmental level makes, participates in making or attempts to use
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision which will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her
economic interests.
3. Yes. The disqualification rules of the Act apply to all public officials in the state
FACTS
You are requesting legal advice about an election in which you a"re interested in
running. As a public official, you are presently a member of the board of directors for the
Pico Water District. This is a special water district governed by Los Angeles County,
located in the City of Pico Rivera.
You may soon consider announcing your candidacy for one of the three upcoming
seats on the El Rancho Unified School Board race scheduled for November 200T.
ANALYSIS
The Act, which applies to any public official in the state, does not prohibit a
public official from holding multiple public positions, either within a single agency or in
different agencies. The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act (Section 87100 et seq.)
only prohibit a public official from participating in a decision that will have a financial
effect on his or her economic interests. I have attached afact sheet entitled, "Holding
Two Positions" for your information.
However, please note that our advice is limited to the Political Reform Act. Other
laws do restrict the ability of public officials to hold two different public offices
simultaneously if the offices are determined to be "incompatible offices." It would be
advisable to contact the Attorney General's Office with respect to the doctrine of
"incompatible offices" and other provisions of law that might apply to your facts.


File No. I-01-171
Page No. 3
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General
By:
Kevin Hall
Legal Intern, Legal Divi sion
KH:jg
Enclosures
